title: Gnaphalium contortum
intro: Gnaphalium contortum, also known as Contorted Cudweed, is a perennial plant species belonging to the Asteraceae family, characterized by its small, daisy-like flowers and contorted stems. Traditionally, Gnaphalium contortum has been used to treat various health conditions, including inflammation, skin irritations, and respiratory issues, due to its potential anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and expectorant properties. The plant's bioactive constituents include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and essential oils, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Gnaphalium contortum is commonly used in herbal preparations, such as infusions, tinctures, and salves, which are used to treat skin conditions, wounds, and respiratory problems.
taxonomy: Gnaphalium contortum is classified within the family Asteraceae, commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. This classification is based on its floral characteristics and growth habits, which resemble other members of the Asteraceae family. At the genus level, Gnaphalium contortum is part of the genus Gnaphalium, a group of small, annual or perennial plants. Within the genus Gnaphalium, Gnaphalium contortum is often distinguished by its unique combination of morphological traits. Further classification may involve its specific taxonomic status, which can vary depending on the source and current botanical understanding.
